verificationNote: Sector-specific requirements are constructed from IEC 62443, ISA-95, ISO 10218 (industrial robots), and NACSA Act 854 obligations for the Malaysian manufacturing sector. Malaysia's semiconductor, electronics, automotive, and food/pharma manufacturing subsectors are covered. These requirements have not been endorsed by MITI, FMM, or NACSA.
nacsaSectorLead: MITI (Ministry of International Trade and Industry)
nacsaSectorNumber: Not explicit NCII sector — subset of Trade, Industry and Economy sector
applicableStandards: IEC 61131-3 (PLC programming languages — relevant for CNC/PLC integrity)
FDA 21 CFR Part 11 (pharmaceutical — computer system validation)
sectorOtCharacteristics: High diversity of OT systems — semiconductor fabs, electronics assembly, automotive, food/beverage, pharmaceutical all have different OT architectures
Industry 4.0 / IIoT adoption increasing OT exposure — direct cloud connectivity of production equipment creates new attack vectors
ISA-95 integration between MES and ERP means production OT is closer to IT than in traditional process industries
Intellectual property (semiconductor process recipes, proprietary formulations) is a high-value target for espionage
Ransomware is the primary threat — EKANS/SNAKE and LockerGoga specifically targeted manufacturing environments
Product quality sabotage risk: manipulation of pharmaceutical batch records or food processing parameters could cause public health harm
High ratio of legacy PLCs and CNC controllers with limited security capabilities

requirements.name: Industrial robot safety and cyber security
requirements.description: Industrial robots (ABB, FANUC, KUKA, Yaskawa) operate in collaborative (cobot) and traditional safety-fenced environments. ISO 10218 and ISO/TS 15066 define physical safety requirements. Cyber security of robot controllers is critical because compromised motion parameters or safety zone overrides could cause physical harm to operators. Robot controllers are increasingly network-connected for remote monitoring and programming.

legal.summary: ISO 10218 mandates safety-rated monitored stop, hand guiding, and safety-monitored speed/separation. If cybersecurity compromise can override these safety functions, then OT security is a prerequisite for ISO 10218 compliance. OSHA Act 514 general safety duties apply.
legal.owner: Automation Engineer / OT Security / Safety Manager

technical.summary: Robot controllers on dedicated production network segment, isolated from enterprise IT. Safety controller (safety PLC or safety-rated robot controller) network-independent of standard controller where architecture permits. Robot programming access restricted to authorised programmers via physical key switch or authenticated engineering station.
actions: Robot controller networks on dedicated VLAN, segregated from enterprise IT and MES via IEC 62443 zone boundary firewall

Safety-rated functions (emergency stop, speed/separation monitoring, safety zones) must not be configurable via standard network interface — use hardware safety modules or safety-rated network protocols only
Robot teach pendant and programming access: physical key switch to enable programming mode; logged access with user identification
Remote monitoring of robot status permitted via IDMZ read-only data export — remote programming access requires VPN with MFA and change management approval
Application whitelisting on robot HMI/controller where operating system supports it (Windows-based controllers)
Firmware and software updates to robot controllers: vendor-approved patches only; tested in staging environment before production deployment; change management approval required

governance.summary: Robot safety and cybersecurity integrated into ISO 10218 safety assessment. Robot programming change management procedure maintained.
actions: Annual robot safety and cybersecurity risk assessment — aligned with ISO 10218 periodic review
Robot program change management: all program changes logged with programmer ID, date, reason, and approval
Vendor remote access to robot controllers documented and controlled via jump server

evidenceItems: Robot controller network architecture diagram (VLAN isolation)
Safety function independence evidence (safety PLC/safety controller separate from network programming interface)
Robot access control configuration (key switch, authentication)

slMapping.sl2: Robot controllers on dedicated VLAN; programming access via key switch; firmware update change management
slMapping.sl3: Safety functions network-independent; application whitelisting on HMI; MFA for remote programming; annual security assessment
slMapping.sl4: Cryptographic integrity verification of robot programs; real-time anomaly detection on robot motion parameters; safety function integrity monitoring

requirements.name: CNC machine security and program integrity
requirements.description: CNC (Computer Numerical Control) machines execute machining programs (G-code) that define precision manufacturing operations. Compromise of CNC programs could cause out-of-specification parts (quality sabotage), equipment damage, or intellectual property theft of proprietary designs. Modern CNC machines are network-connected for DNC (Distributed Numerical Control) program transfer and remote monitoring.

legal.summary: CNC networks are OT assets. If NCII-designated, NACSA s18 measures apply. CNC program integrity is critical for product quality and intellectual property protection.
legal.owner: CNC Programming Manager / OT Security / Quality Manager

technical.summary: CNC machines on dedicated shop floor network. DNC server (program distribution) in IDMZ with controlled access. CNC program library with version control and integrity verification. No direct internet access from CNC machines.
actions: CNC machines on dedicated shop floor VLAN — no direct connectivity to enterprise IT or internet
DNC server for program distribution: located in IDMZ; access controlled by user authentication; programs transferred via secure file share (not open network shares)
CNC program version control: maintain master program library with checksums (SHA-256); verify program integrity before production run
Intellectual property protection: CNC programs containing proprietary designs classified as confidential; access restricted to authorised programmers only
USB port control on CNC machines: disable or restrict USB to prevent unauthorised program loading or data exfiltration
Legacy CNC machines without network security: compensating controls are physical isolation and manual program loading via verified media

governance.summary: CNC program management integrated with quality management system (ISO 9001). Program change control documented.
actions: CNC program change control: ISO 9001-aligned document control for machining programs
Annual review of CNC network architecture and access controls
IP-sensitive programs: additional access restrictions and audit trail requirements

evidenceItems: DNC server configuration and access control
CNC program version control and checksum register
USB port control policy and configuration

slMapping.sl2: CNC machines on dedicated VLAN; DNC server with authentication; USB port controls; program version control
slMapping.sl3: Program integrity verification before production; IP classification and access restriction; legacy CNC exceptions documented
slMapping.sl4: Cryptographic signing of CNC programs; automated integrity verification at machine before execution; anomaly detection on machining parameters

requirements.name: SCADA/MES integration security (ISA-95 boundary)
requirements.description: Manufacturing Execution Systems (MES) bridge the gap between enterprise ERP systems and shop floor control (SCADA/PLCs). The ISA-95 model defines integration levels. The MES-to-SCADA boundary is a critical security boundary — compromise of MES could allow manipulation of production schedules, recipe parameters, or quality records. Industry 4.0 initiatives often weaken this boundary by introducing direct cloud-to-OT connectivity.

legal.summary: ISA-95 Levels 0-3 (production) to Level 4 (enterprise) boundary is a critical security demarcation. IEC 62443 zone/conduit model applies. Industry 4.0 cloud connectivity must not bypass this boundary.
legal.owner: OT Security Manager / MES Administrator / IT Security

technical.summary: MES in IDMZ between enterprise IT and production OT. All data exchange between MES and SCADA/PLCs via controlled interfaces with input validation. No direct ERP-to-PLC communication. Industry 4.0 cloud connectivity via IDMZ gateway only — not direct OT-to-cloud.
actions: MES servers in IDMZ — not on production OT network and not on enterprise IT network
MES-to-SCADA/PLC communication: defined interfaces only; input validation on all production parameters received from MES before executing on production equipment
No direct ERP-to-PLC communication — all production scheduling and recipe data flows through MES with validation
Industry 4.0 / IIoT connectivity: all cloud connections via IDMZ gateway with data diode or proxy; no direct OT-to-cloud paths
MES access control: role-based access; production operators cannot modify recipes or schedules without supervisor approval
MES database integrity: production records (batch records, quality data) protected against unauthorised modification; audit trail maintained

governance.summary: ISA-95 boundary security reviewed annually. Industry 4.0 initiatives require security assessment before OT connectivity changes.
actions: Annual review of ISA-95 boundary architecture — verify no unapproved IT-to-OT paths
Industry 4.0 / IIoT project security assessment: mandatory OT security review before any new cloud-to-OT connectivity
MES access control review quarterly — remove stale accounts

evidenceItems: ISA-95 boundary architecture diagram (MES in IDMZ)
MES-to-SCADA interface definition and input validation configuration
Industry 4.0 connectivity inventory (all cloud-to-OT paths documented)
MES access control configuration and review records

slMapping.sl2: MES in IDMZ; defined MES-to-SCADA interfaces; no direct ERP-to-PLC; MES access control
slMapping.sl3: Input validation on all MES-to-PLC parameters; Industry 4.0 via IDMZ gateway only; MES audit trail; quarterly access review
slMapping.sl4: Data diode for OT-to-cloud telemetry; cryptographic integrity of production records; real-time anomaly detection on production parameters

requirements.name: Supply chain integrity for OT components
requirements.description: Manufacturing OT systems rely on controllers, firmware, and software from global supply chains. Counterfeit PLCs, compromised firmware, or tampered software updates could introduce backdoors or cause equipment malfunction. The SolarWinds and 3CX supply chain attacks demonstrated the effectiveness of this vector. For semiconductor manufacturers, the integrity of manufacturing equipment itself (lithography, CVD, etch tools) is critical.

legal.summary: IEC 62443-2-4 requires supply chain security for system integrators. NACSA s22 (codes of practice) may impose supply chain requirements on NCII operators. Semiconductor manufacturers have additional export control obligations.

technical.summary: Maintain approved vendor list for OT components. Verify firmware/software integrity via vendor-provided checksums before deployment. Test all OT software updates in staging environment. Restrict supply chain to authorised distributors.
actions: Maintain approved vendor list for PLCs, HMIs, drives, and other OT components — purchase only from authorised distributors
Firmware and software integrity: verify vendor-provided checksums (SHA-256 or vendor signing) before deployment to production OT
OT software updates tested in staging/lab environment before production deployment — minimum 48-hour staging period for non-emergency patches
Semiconductor process equipment: vendor access for maintenance strictly controlled; all vendor sessions monitored and recorded
Vendor security assessment: evaluate critical OT vendors against IEC 62443-4-1 product security requirements
Track end-of-life (EOL) announcements for all OT components — plan migration before security support ends

governance.summary: OT supply chain security integrated into vendor management process. Vendor security assessments documented.
actions: Annual vendor security assessment for Tier 1 OT suppliers
EOL register maintained for all OT components with planned migration dates
Supply chain incident response: procedure for responding to vendor-reported vulnerabilities or compromised updates

evidenceItems: OT patch staging and testing records

slMapping.sl2: Approved vendor list; firmware integrity verification; staging environment for updates; EOL tracking
slMapping.sl3: Vendor security assessments (IEC 62443-4-1); monitored vendor access; supply chain incident response procedure
slMapping.sl4: Cryptographic chain-of-custody for OT software from vendor to production; automated integrity monitoring of deployed OT firmware

requirements.name: Quality management system (QMS) and OT security integration
requirements.description: ISO 9001 quality management systems depend on the integrity of production data, measurement systems, and process parameters. For pharmaceutical manufacturers (FDA 21 CFR Part 11, GAMP 5) and food manufacturers (HACCP, GMP), data integrity is a regulatory requirement. Cyber compromise of quality-related OT systems (measurement instruments, batch records, calibration data) could result in regulatory violations, product recalls, or public health harm.

legal.summary: FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requires electronic record integrity, audit trails, and electronic signatures for pharmaceutical manufacturers exporting to the US. ISO 9001 requires control of monitoring and measuring equipment. OT security is a prerequisite for data integrity compliance.
legal.owner: Quality Manager / OT Security / Regulatory Compliance

technical.summary: Quality-critical OT systems (measurement instruments, batch record systems, calibration databases) protected with integrity controls, audit trails, and access management. Electronic records comply with ALCOA+ principles (Attributable, Legible, Contemporaneous, Original, Accurate).
actions: Quality measurement instruments (CMMs, spectrometers, scales): network-connected instruments on dedicated quality network segment with access control
Electronic batch records: complete audit trail — all changes tracked with user ID, timestamp, old value, new value; no deletion of records permitted
Electronic signatures on batch release: compliant with FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requirements where applicable; user authentication before signature
Calibration data integrity: calibration records stored in protected database; calibration certificates verified against instrument serial numbers
Quality data backup: quality-critical data backed up to separate storage; backup integrity verified; retention per regulatory requirements (pharmaceutical: minimum 1 year beyond product expiry)
SCADA historian data used for quality trending: historian database integrity protected; access controlled to prevent retrospective modification

governance.summary: QMS-OT security integration documented in quality manual. Data integrity risk assessment performed annually.
actions: Data integrity risk assessment: annual review of quality-critical OT systems for data integrity risks
Quality-OT security procedure: defines responsibilities for OT security aspects of QMS
Regulatory audit preparation: maintain evidence of OT security controls supporting data integrity for regulatory inspections (FDA, GMP auditors)

evidenceItems: Electronic batch record audit trail configuration
Electronic signature configuration (21 CFR Part 11 compliance where applicable)

slMapping.sl2: Quality instruments on dedicated network; electronic batch records with audit trail; calibration data protected
slMapping.sl3: 21 CFR Part 11 compliant electronic signatures; SCADA historian integrity protection; annual data integrity risk assessment
slMapping.sl4: Cryptographic integrity of quality records; real-time anomaly detection on measurement data; automated reconciliation between instrument readings and batch records

requirements.name: Semiconductor fabrication equipment security
requirements.description: Semiconductor fab equipment (CVD, etch, lithography, CMP, ion implantation) executes process recipes that define wafer fabrication steps. These recipes are extremely valuable IP — a single advanced process node recipe set represents billions in R&D investment. Equipment is increasingly network-connected for factory automation (SEMI standards). Compromise could destroy wafer lots, steal process IP, or disrupt production. SEMI E187 defines cybersecurity requirements for semiconductor manufacturing equipment.

legal.summary: Semiconductor fab equipment is OT. Process recipes are trade secrets — both IP protection and process integrity require OT security. SEMI E187 provides equipment-level cybersecurity requirements.
legal.owner: Fab Engineering Manager / OT Security / IT Security

technical.summary: Fab equipment on dedicated process network segments by bay/area. AMHS (Automated Material Handling) on separate network. Equipment vendor remote access via IDMZ jump server only. Process recipe management system with version control and access restriction.
actions: Fab equipment network segmented by process area (lithography, etch, CVD, CMP) on dedicated VLANs
AMHS (Automated Material Handling System) on separate VLAN from process equipment — AMHS compromise must not affect process control
Process recipe management: version-controlled recipe library with access restricted to authorised process engineers; all recipe changes logged with engineer ID, timestamp, and approval
Equipment vendor remote access: IDMZ jump server with MFA; all sessions recorded; vendor access time-limited and requires fab engineering approval
SEMI E187 compliance: require cybersecurity capability assessment for all new equipment purchases; include cybersecurity requirements in equipment procurement specifications
Gas and chemical delivery system controllers: on safety-critical process network; setpoint changes require supervisor approval; independent high/low alarms
Metrology data integrity: measurement data used for statistical process control (SPC) must be protected against tampering; anomalous SPC data triggers investigation

governance.summary: Semiconductor fab OT security integrated with equipment qualification and process control procedures.
actions: Annual fab OT security assessment — cover all equipment network segments
Equipment cybersecurity assessment at procurement — SEMI E187 compliance verification
Process recipe access review quarterly — remove access for engineers who have changed roles

evidenceItems: Fab network architecture diagram by process area
Process recipe management system access control and change log
SEMI E187 compliance assessment for new equipment

slMapping.sl2: Equipment on dedicated VLANs by area; AMHS separated; recipe version control; vendor access via IDMZ
slMapping.sl3: SEMI E187 compliance; recipe access restricted and logged; gas/chemical system independent alarms; SPC data integrity protection
slMapping.sl4: Cryptographic integrity of process recipes; real-time anomaly detection on equipment parameters; automated recipe integrity verification before lot processing

requirements.name: Automated Guided Vehicle (AGV) and Autonomous Mobile Robot (AMR) security
requirements.description: AGVs and AMRs are used extensively in semiconductor fabs (AMHS), automotive plants, and warehouses. These autonomous vehicles navigate shared spaces with human workers, creating safety implications if compromised. Fleet management systems control routing, traffic management, and charging. Compromise could cause collisions, production disruption, or be used as a network pivot point.

legal.summary: AGVs/AMRs are safety-relevant OT systems operating in shared human-machine spaces. ISO 3691-4 defines safety requirements for driverless industrial trucks. Cybersecurity compromise of AGV/AMR systems has worker safety implications under OSHA Act 514.
legal.owner: Automation Engineer / OT Security / Safety Manager

technical.summary: AGV/AMR fleet management system on dedicated network segment. Safety systems (obstacle detection, emergency stop) operate independently of fleet management network. Vehicle-to-infrastructure communication secured.
actions: AGV/AMR fleet management system on dedicated VLAN — isolated from production OT and enterprise IT
Safety systems (LiDAR obstacle detection, emergency stop, safety bumpers) must operate independently of fleet management network — hardware safety circuits
Vehicle-to-infrastructure (V2I) wireless communication: dedicated SSID with WPA3 Enterprise; no shared wireless with other systems
AGV/AMR firmware updates: vendor-approved updates only; tested in controlled environment before fleet deployment; staged rollout
Fleet management server: access controlled; route and map changes logged; no unauthorised route modifications
Charging station controllers: on same dedicated VLAN as fleet management; access controlled

governance.summary: AGV/AMR security integrated with safety management system. Fleet management access reviewed quarterly.
actions: Annual AGV/AMR safety and cybersecurity assessment — verify safety system independence from network
Fleet management access review quarterly
AGV/AMR incident investigation: any unexpected vehicle behaviour investigated for both safety and cybersecurity causes

evidenceItems: Fleet management access control and change log

slMapping.sl2: Fleet management on dedicated VLAN; dedicated wireless SSID; safety systems independent; firmware update process
slMapping.sl3: WPA3 Enterprise for V2I; fleet route change management; annual safety-cybersecurity assessment; staged firmware rollout
slMapping.sl4: Encrypted V2I communication; real-time anomaly detection on vehicle behaviour; cryptographic firmware integrity verification

requirements.name: Paint shop and environmental control system security
requirements.description: Automotive and manufacturing paint shops have stringent environmental control requirements (temperature, humidity, air flow, solvent concentration). Paint shop OT systems control HVAC, solvent recovery, electrostatic spray equipment, and oven/curing systems. Solvent vapour concentration monitoring is safety-critical — explosive atmosphere risk (ATEX). Environmental compliance (VOC emissions) depends on OT system integrity.

legal.summary: Paint shop OT controls safety-critical environmental parameters. Solvent concentration monitoring prevents explosive atmospheres. VOC emission monitoring ensures Environmental Quality Act compliance. ATEX compliance depends on OT system integrity.
legal.owner: Paint Shop Manager / OT Security / Safety Manager / Environmental Compliance

technical.summary: Paint shop environmental control on dedicated OT network. Solvent concentration monitoring with independent safety interlocks. VOC emission monitoring data integrity protected for regulatory compliance.
actions: Paint shop environmental control (HVAC, solvent recovery, booth ventilation) on dedicated OT network segment
Solvent vapour concentration monitoring: independent safety interlocks (hardware-based LEL monitoring) that operate independently of BMS/SCADA
Electrostatic spray equipment grounding and safety interlocks: not network-controllable — hardware safety circuits only
Oven/curing zone temperature control: independent over-temperature alarms with automatic shutdown
VOC emission monitoring: Continuous Emission Monitoring System (CEMS) data integrity protected; tamper-evident audit trail for regulatory reporting
Paint recipe management: colour formula and spray parameter recipes version-controlled; access restricted to paint process engineers

governance.summary: Paint shop OT security integrated with ATEX compliance and environmental management system.
actions: Annual paint shop OT security and safety review — verify independence of safety interlocks
Environmental monitoring data integrity audit — verify CEMS data accuracy and tamper protection
Paint recipe access review — quarterly

evidenceItems: Paint recipe version control and access log
ATEX compliance documentation for network-connected equipment

slMapping.sl2: Dedicated paint shop OT network; independent LEL monitoring; CEMS data protected; recipe version control
slMapping.sl3: Hardware safety interlocks independent of network; CEMS tamper-evident audit trail; annual OT security-safety review
slMapping.sl4: Real-time anomaly detection on environmental parameters; cryptographic integrity of emission records; automated cross-verification of safety system status

subsectors.name: Semiconductor Fabrication
subsectors.description: Semiconductor fabrication (wafer fab) environments use highly specialised OT systems for CVD, etch, lithography, CMP, ion implantation, and metrology. Process recipes are extremely valuable intellectual property. Contamination or parameter deviation at any process step can destroy entire wafer lots worth millions of ringgit. Malaysia's Penang and Kulim Hi-Tech Park are major global semiconductor manufacturing hubs.
nacsaRequirements: Process recipe classification as confidential IP — access restricted to authorised process engineers
Equipment vendor remote access via IDMZ jump server only — all sessions recorded
SEMI E187 (semiconductor manufacturing equipment cybersecurity) compliance for new equipment procurement
Cleanroom environmental data integrity — contamination events require accurate historical data for root cause analysis

subsectors.name: Food and Pharmaceutical Manufacturing
subsectors.description: Food and pharmaceutical manufacturing have unique regulatory requirements for data integrity (FDA 21 CFR Part 11, GAMP 5, HACCP, GMP). Batch manufacturing control systems must maintain complete audit trails. Product quality sabotage through parameter manipulation could cause public health harm. Cold chain monitoring integrity is critical for biologics and temperature-sensitive products.
nacsaRequirements: Electronic batch record integrity — ALCOA+ principles (Attributable, Legible, Contemporaneous, Original, Accurate)
21 CFR Part 11 compliant electronic signatures where pharmaceutical export to US market
HACCP critical control point monitoring — OT integrity of CCP sensors and alarms
Traceability system integrity — lot tracking for recall capability
GMP deviation management — any OT security incident that may affect product quality triggers deviation investigation

subsectors.name: Automotive and Discrete Manufacturing
subsectors.description: Automotive and discrete manufacturing relies heavily on industrial robots, CNC machines, conveyor systems, and automated guided vehicles (AGVs). Production line stoppages have massive financial impact (automotive OEMs can lose >RM1 million per hour of unplanned downtime). Paint shop environmental control, welding robot coordination, and body-in-white dimensional accuracy are safety and quality critical.
otSystems: Paint shop environmental and robot controllers
nacsaRequirements: Robot safety controller independence from standard network — ISO 10218 compliance
AGV/AMR fleet management system network isolation — autonomous vehicle safety
Vision inspection system integrity — quality-critical measurement data
Production line OT network resilience — no single point of failure for line-stop conditions
Vendor remote access for robot programming — controlled via jump server with MFA and change management

sectorSpecificThreats.threat: Product quality sabotage via batch parameter manipulation
Intellectual property theft (semiconductor process recipes, proprietary designs)
Supply chain compromise of OT components or firmware
